title: Has Britain's Budget Watchdog Become a 'Straitjacket on Growth'?
description: Ahead of the UK Budget, critics argue the Office for Budget Responsibility holds too much power over government economic policy.
lede: As the UK prepares for Wednesday's Budget, a fierce debate is brewing over the role of the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR). Critics, including a former Labour Cabinet minister and the Trades Union Congress, argue the influential department has become a "straitjacket on growth" and is essentially dictating government ambition.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: A team sitting in a far corner of the Ministry of Justice building is drawing significant attention for its extraordinary influence over UK economic policy. The Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), staffed mainly by young intellectuals and civil servants, assesses the cost of government policies and monitors whether the chancellor is meeting fiscal rules.

Recently, the Trades Union Congress described the "unaccountable OBR" as a "straitjacket on growth." Lou Haigh, a former Labour Cabinet minister, called it an "unelected institution dictating the limits of government ambition."
The scrutiny comes just ahead of Wednesday's Budget, raising questions about whether the OBR's chairman, Richard Hughes, holds as much sway as the Chancellor herself.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The OBR wields immense power over the UK's financial trajectory. At the end of October, it reportedly lowered its forecast for productivity by 0.3 percentage points. According to the Institute for Fiscal Studies, such a downgrade could add £21 billion to the Budget black hole by 2029-30.

These forecasts severely limit the government's ability to enact policies like tax cuts or large-scale spending. When Labour entered office, it passed a new law giving the OBR even more power to initiate forecasts independently, a direct response to the market turmoil caused by the Conservatives' uncosted mini-Budget in September 2022.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The OBR was born out of the 2008 financial crisis, stemming from an obscure Conservative Party pamphlet titled "Reconstruction." It was formally established by the coalition government in 2010 to bring discipline and transparency to fiscal policymaking.

However, some fear this independence has gone too far. Paul Johnson, former director of the Institute for Fiscal Studies, warned against a "more powerful fiscal technocracy," noting that choices over taxing and spending are inherently political, not just technical questions.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Despite the criticism, OBR Chairman Richard Hughes remains steadfast, insisting the body's powers are strictly limited to those granted by Parliament. He emphasizes that the chancellor ultimately chooses both the policies and the fiscal rules.

As the Chancellor prepares to deliver the Budget, the tension between the government's growth ambitions and the OBR's rigorous forecasts will be on full display, highlighting an ongoing struggle over who truly steers the UK economy.
